slides.title: The longest-lived people stop at *80 percent full*.
slides.sub: It is a gentle habit, not a diet. Here is the simple idea behind eating a little less.

slides.title: Stop at 80 percent
slides.body: In Okinawa, people practice *hara hachi bu*: stopping when they are roughly 80 percent full, not stuffed.

slides.title: Overeating adds up
slides.body: Eating past full, meal after meal, *raises metabolic load* over the years. Small surpluses quietly accumulate.

slides.title: Fullness signals lag
slides.body: Your gut takes about *twenty minutes* to tell your brain you are full. Slowing down lets the signal catch up.

slides.title: Lead with protein
slides.body: Start with protein and fiber, and use *smaller plates*. You feel satisfied on a little less, naturally.

slides.title: Gentle, not strict
slides.body: This is *a calm habit, not deprivation*. You leave the table comfortable, never hungry or counting.

slides.title: Make better habits *easy to see*.
slides.body: Vibe Health turns your food, sleep and movement into one simple daily plan, so eating well becomes a calm routine.
slides.cta_text: search ‘Vibe Health’ on the App Store

caption:
Some of the longest-lived people on earth share one quiet habit: they stop eating at about 80 percent full. 🍽️

It is called hara hachi bu. Slow down, lead with protein and fiber, use smaller plates. A gentle habit, not deprivation. 🔖

youtube_title: Why the Longest-Lived People Stop Eating at 80 Percent Full
